concepts.title: Between Attention Layers: The Feed-Forward Network
concepts.content:
Key ideas:
- applied independently to each position
- d_ff=2048 inner dimension (4× d_model)
- ReLU activation between two linear layers
- same weights across positions, different per layer

After multi-head attention, each position's representation passes through a two-layer feed-forward network applied independently:

  FFN(x) = max(0, xW₁ + b₁)W₂ + b₂

With d_model=512 → d_ff=2048 → d_model=512. The inner layer expands to 2048 (4×), applies ReLU, then contracts back.

Why is this needed at all? Attention is a weighted average — it's essentially a linear operation over values. Without non-linearity, stacking attention layers would be equivalent to one layer. The FFN injects the non-linearity that lets the model represent complex functions.

The same FFN weights are shared across all sequence positions within a layer (processing each position identically), but each of the N=6 layers has its own distinct FFN weights. Think of attention as deciding 'who to listen to' and FFN as deciding 'what to think about what you heard.'

concepts.title: The Encoder: Six Layers of Self-Attention
concepts.content:
Key ideas:
- N=6 identical layers
- self-attention: every token attends to every other
- FFN applied after attention in each layer
- output is a sequence of contextual representations

The encoder is a stack of N=6 identical layers. Each layer has two sub-layers: (1) multi-head self-attention and (2) position-wise FFN, each wrapped with residual + LayerNorm.

In self-attention, every position attends to every other position in the input. After 6 layers, each token's representation is a rich contextual blend of the entire input sequence. The word 'bank' in 'river bank' and 'bank account' will have very different representations after 6 layers because of what surrounds it.

The encoder processes all input tokens in parallel — no sequential dependency. This is the key training efficiency gain over RNNs. The output is a matrix of shape (seq_len × d_model=512) — one rich vector per input position — that the decoder will later query via cross-attention.

concepts.title: Keeping Gradients Alive: Residual + LayerNorm
concepts.content:
Key ideas:
- residual: output = x + Sublayer(x)
- prevents gradient vanishing in deep nets
- LayerNorm normalizes across feature dimension
- applied after each sub-layer in every encoder/decoder layer

With N=6 layers and multiple sub-layers per layer, training a deep Transformer without tricks is hard. Two solutions work together:

**Residual connections** (from He et al., 2016 ResNets): output = x + Sublayer(x). The '+x' shortcut gives gradients a direct highway back to earlier layers during backpropagation. Instead of learning the full transformation, each sub-layer only needs to learn the residual (the difference from identity).

**Layer normalization** (Ba et al., 2016): normalizes across the feature dimension (d_model) for each position independently. Unlike batch normalization, it doesn't depend on batch size, making it ideal for variable-length sequences in NLP.

Together they stabilize training and make depth practical. Every single sub-layer in the Transformer uses: LayerNorm(x + Sublayer(x)).

concepts.title: Two Flavors of Attention: Self vs Cross
concepts.content:
Key ideas:
- self-attention: Q/K/V from same sequence
- cross-attention: Q from decoder, K/V from encoder
- three uses in the Transformer
- cross-attention bridges encoder and decoder

The Transformer uses attention in three distinct ways:

1. **Encoder self-attention**: Q, K, V all come from the encoder input. Each token attends to all other input tokens.
2. **Decoder masked self-attention**: Q, K, V all come from the decoder's own previous outputs, but with a causal mask so position i can't see positions j>i.
3. **Decoder cross-attention**: Q comes from the decoder, but K and V come from the encoder's output. This is how the decoder reads the source sentence.

Think of cross-attention as a question-and-answer session: the decoder has a question (Q) about what to generate next, and the encoder holds the answers (K and V) about the source sentence. The decoder selectively reads the most relevant parts of the encoder's memory for each generation step.

concepts.title: No Peeking: Causal Masking in the Decoder
concepts.content:
Key ideas:
- set future positions to -∞ before softmax
- prevents attending to future tokens
- enforces autoregressive property
- only needed in decoder self-attention (not cross-attention)

During training, the decoder receives the target sequence shifted right (teacher forcing). Without masking, position 5 could attend to positions 6, 7, 8... — essentially cheating by reading the answer. At inference time, those future tokens don't exist yet.

The fix is elegant: before softmax, set all positions j > i to -∞ in the attention score matrix. After softmax, e^(-∞) = 0, so those positions get zero weight. Position i can only attend to positions ≤ i.

The encoder doesn't need this because it processes the full input simultaneously — there's no generation step, no future to hide. The encoder's job is to build rich contextual representations of all input tokens together, so attending everywhere is correct and desirable.

concepts.title: The Decoder: Generating One Token at a Time
concepts.content:
Key ideas:
- N=6 layers with three sub-layers each
- layer 1: masked self-attention
- layer 2: cross-attention to encoder
- layer 3: FFN
- final linear + softmax gives vocabulary probabilities

The decoder mirrors the encoder's 6-layer depth but each layer has three sub-layers instead of two:

1. **Masked multi-head self-attention** — the decoder attends to its own previously generated tokens (with causal mask)
2. **Multi-head cross-attention** — the decoder queries the encoder's output to gather source information
3. **Position-wise FFN** — non-linear transformation

Each sub-layer is again wrapped with residual + LayerNorm.

After the final decoder layer, a learned linear projection maps from d_model=512 to vocabulary size (~37,000 for WMT EN-DE), followed by softmax to produce the probability distribution over the next token. The highest-probability token is selected (or beam search explores multiple candidates). This is how the Transformer generates output sequences token by token.

quiz.question: What is the correct order of sub-layers in each Transformer decoder layer? Answer with a number.
